question: He said, 'If I were a bird, I would fly to you.'
options: He said that if he were a bird, he would fly to me.
He said that if he had been a bird, he would fly to me.
He said that if he were a bird, he would have flown to me.
He told that if he was a bird, he would fly to me.
answer: He said that if he were a bird, he would fly to me.
explanation: In hypothetical/unreal conditions (Type 2), the 'were' and 'would' usually remain unchanged in indirect speech to preserve the imaginary sense. ✅

question: She said, 'If I had seen him, I would have told him.'
options: She said that if she had seen him, she would have told him.
She said that if she saw him, she would have told him.
She said that she would have told him if she saw him.
She told that had she seen him, she would tell him.
answer: She said that if she had seen him, she would have told him.
explanation: In Type 3 conditionals (Past Unreal), the tense is already in Past Perfect, so it remains unchanged. ✅

question: He said, 'I must go now.'
options: He said that he must go then.
He said that he had to go then.
He said that he would have to go then.
Both A and B are correct.
answer: Both A and B are correct.
explanation: 'Must' can remain 'must' or change to 'had to' (for immediate necessity) or 'would have to' (for future necessity). ✅

question: She said, 'You should work hard.'
options: She said that I should work hard.
She said that I should have worked hard.
She advised that I work hard.
Both A and C are correct.
answer: Both A and C are correct.
explanation: 'Should' for advice remains 'should' or can be reported using the verb 'advised'. ✅

question: He said, 'I could swim when I was young.'
options: He said that he could swim when he was young.
He said that he could have swum when he was young.
He said that he was able to swim when he was young.
Both A and C are correct.
answer: Both A and C are correct.
explanation: 'Could' expressing past ability can remain 'could' or change to 'was able to'. ✅

question: She said, 'It is time we left.'
options: She said that it was time they left.
She said that it was time they had left.
She said that it is time they left.
She told that it was time they should leave.
answer: She said that it was time they had left.
explanation: After 'It is time', the simple past 'left' (subjunctive) changes to past perfect 'had left' in indirect speech. ✅

question: The boy said, 'I would rather starve than beg.'
options: The boy said that he would rather starve than beg.
The boy said that he would have rather starved than begged.
The boy told that he preferred starving to begging.
Both A and C are correct.
answer: The boy said that he would rather starve than beg.
explanation: 'Would rather' remains unchanged in indirect speech. ✅

question: He said, 'I used to go to the temple every morning.'
options: He said that he used to go to the temple every morning.
He said that he had used to go to the temple every morning.
He told that he was used to going to the temple.
He said that I used to go to the temple.
answer: He said that he used to go to the temple every morning.
explanation: 'Used to' remains unchanged as it denotes a past habitual action. ✅

question: She said, 'If I win the lottery, I will buy a car.'
options: She said that if she won the lottery, she would buy a car.
She said that if she wins the lottery, she will buy a car.
She said that if she would win the lottery, she will buy a car.
She told that she would buy a car if she had won the lottery.
answer: She said that if she won the lottery, she would buy a car.
explanation: In Type 1 conditionals, Simple Present changes to Simple Past, and 'will' changes to 'would'. ✅

question: He said, 'You ought to be more careful.'
options: He said that I ought to be more careful.
He said that I ought to have been more careful.
He advised me that I ought to be more careful.
Both A and C are correct.
answer: Both A and C are correct.
explanation: 'Ought to' remains unchanged in indirect speech. ✅

question: He said, 'I might visit you if I have time.'
options: He said that he might visit me if he had time.
He said that he might visit me if he has time.
He told that he might visit me if he might have time.
He said that he might visit you if he had time.
answer: He said that he might visit me if he had time.
explanation: 'Might' remains 'might', but the 'if' clause tense (have) changes to past (had). ✅

question: She said, 'Need I attend the meeting?'
options: She asked if she had to attend the meeting.
She asked if she need attend the meeting.
She enquired whether it was necessary for her to attend.
Both A and C are correct.
answer: Both A and C are correct.
explanation: Interrogative 'need' as a modal usually changes to 'had to' or 'was necessary'. ✅

question: He said, 'I had better leave before it rains.'
options: He said that he had better leave before it rained.
He said that he had better have left before it rained.
He told that it was better if he left before it rained.
Both A and C are correct.
answer: He said that he had better leave before it rained.
explanation: 'Had better' remains unchanged, but the subordinate clause 'rains' changes to 'rained'. ✅

question: The teacher said, 'If you study, you pass.'
options: The teacher said that if you study, you pass.
The teacher said that if I studied, I would pass.
The teacher said that if I study, I pass.
Both A and C are correct.
answer: Both A and C are correct.
explanation: For zero conditionals (general truths), the tense can remain present or shift to past. ✅

question: She said, 'My father said, "Honesty is the best policy."'
options: She said that her father had said that honesty is the best policy.
She said that her father said that honesty is the best policy.
She told that her father said honesty was the best policy.
Both A and B are correct.
answer: Both A and B are correct.
explanation: Double narration: Tense changes for the first reporting, but not for the universal truth. ✅

question: He said, 'I should have reached by now.'
options: He said that he should have reached by then.
He said that he should reached by then.
He told that he must have reached by then.
He said that he should have been reaching by then.
answer: He said that he should have reached by then.
explanation: 'Should have + V3' remains unchanged except for the time expression 'now' to 'then'. ✅

question: The lady said, 'If I had a car, I would drive to the office.'
options: The lady said that if she had a car, she would drive to the office.
The lady said that if she had had a car, she would drive to the office.
The lady told that if she had a car, she would have driven.
The lady said if I had a car, I would drive to the office.
answer: The lady said that if she had a car, she would drive to the office.
explanation: Type 2 conditional (Hypothetical) remains in its original form. ✅

question: He said, 'We must love our neighbors.'
options: He said that we must love our neighbors.
He said that they had to love their neighbors.
He said that they should love their neighbors.
Both A and C are correct.
answer: Both A and C are correct.
explanation: When 'must' expresses a permanent moral duty, it remains 'must' or 'should'. ✅

question: She said, 'Would you like some tea?'
options: She offered me some tea.
She asked if I would like some tea.
She asked whether I would have liked some tea.
Both A and B are correct.
answer: Both A and B are correct.
explanation: A question with 'Would you like' is an offer and can be reported either way. ✅

question: He said, 'I shall be eighteen next month.'
options: He said that he would be eighteen the following month.
He said that he should be eighteen the following month.
He said that he shall be eighteen next month.
He told that he will be eighteen the following month.
answer: He said that he would be eighteen the following month.
explanation: 'Shall' for future fact changes to 'would' for 3rd person 'he'. ✅

question: I said, 'I must have left my keys in the car.'
options: I said that I must have left my keys in the car.
I said that I had had to leave my keys in the car.
I said that I must leave my keys in the car.
I told that I might have left my keys in the car.
answer: I said that I must have left my keys in the car.
explanation: 'Must have + V3' (Logical conclusion) remains unchanged. ✅

question: She said, 'He can't have done it.'
options: She said that he couldn't have done it.
She said that he can't have done it.
She said that he couldn't do it.
Both A and B are correct.
answer: Both A and B are correct.
explanation: Deductions about the past can stay in the present or shift to 'couldn't have'. ✅

question: He said, 'I wish I had been there.'
options: He wished that he had been there.
He wished that he were there.
He said that he wished he was there.
He wished that he had had been there.
answer: He wished that he had been there.
explanation: Past perfect 'had been' in a wish remains unchanged. ✅

question: The captain said, 'If we had played well, we would have won.'
options: The captain said that if they had played well, they would have won.
The captain said that if they played well, they would have won.
The captain told that if they had played well, they should have won.
The captain said if we had played well, we would have won.
answer: The captain said that if they had played well, they would have won.
explanation: Type 3 conditional remains unchanged except for pronouns. ✅

question: He said, 'I should like to be a doctor.'
options: He said that he would like to be a doctor.
He said that he should like to be a doctor.
He told that he wanted to be a doctor.
Both A and B are correct.
answer: Both A and B are correct.
explanation: 'Should' for preference/desire often changes to 'would' for the third person. ✅

question: She said, 'If it rains, the match will be cancelled.'
options: She said that if it rained, the match would be cancelled.
She said that if it rains, the match will be cancelled.
She told that if it rained, the match will be cancelled.
She said that if it would rain, the match would be cancelled.
answer: She said that if it rained, the match would be cancelled.
explanation: Type 1 conditional: Present ➔ Past, Will ➔ Would. ✅

question: I said, 'May I go out?'
options: I asked if I might go out.
I asked if I may go out.
I told that I might go out.
I requested if I might go out.
answer: I asked if I might go out.
explanation: May ➔ Might in questions for permission. ✅

question: He said to me, 'What would you do if you were in my place?'
options: He asked me what I would do if I were in his place.
He asked me what would I do if I were in his place.
He asked me what I would have done if I were in his place.
He told me what I would do if I was in his place.
answer: He asked me what I would do if I were in his place.
explanation: Wh- question word order + Type 2 conditional remains 'would/were'. ✅

question: She said, 'He might have been sleeping.'
options: She said that he might have been sleeping.
She said that he might be sleeping.
She told that he might have slept.
She said that he could have been sleeping.
answer: She said that he might have been sleeping.
explanation: 'Might have been + ing' remains unchanged. ✅

question: He said, 'Suppose you were invited, would you go?'
options: He asked whether I would go if I were invited.
He asked me to suppose I was invited and if I would go.
He asked if I were invited, would I go.
Both A and B are correct.
answer: Both A and B are correct.
explanation: Reporting 'Suppose' sentences involves turning them into conditional 'if' structures. ✅
